code-1.title: Insert a new inventory item
code-1.prompt: Add a new row to the `inventory_items` table for an item named 'Student Badge' in the 'Accessories' category, priced at 2.50. Set status to 'active', is_test to 0, notes to NULL, and last_updated to '2026-02-10'. Use an explicit column list and do not specify the id.
code-1.hint: Focus on the SQL task being asked for, not on copying final query text.

help.concept: Build the query from the operation the exercise is testing.
help.hint_1: Think about which clauses or functions are required for the task.
help.hint_2: Construct the query based on what result the exercise expects, not by repeating exact solution wording.

code-2.title: Insert with default values
code-2.prompt: Insert a new item called 'Sample Lanyard' in the 'Accessories' category, priced at 5.00. Only specify name, category, and price. Let all other columns use their defaults.
code-2.hint: Focus on the SQL task being asked for, not on copying final query text.

help.concept: Build the query from the operation the exercise is testing.
help.hint_1: Think about which clauses or functions are required for the task.
help.hint_2: Construct the query based on what result the exercise expects, not by repeating exact solution wording.

code-2.starterCode: -- Insert only name, category, and price

code-3.title: Insert multiple rows
code-3.prompt:
Insert two new rows into `inventory_items` in a single statement:
1. 'Test Eraser', 'Accessories', 1.25, status 'draft', is_test 1, notes 'Test batch', last_updated '2026-02-11'
2. 'Trial Pen', 'Accessories', 1.50, status 'draft', is_test 1, notes 'Trial batch', last_updated '2026-02-11'
Do not specify id. Use an explicit column list.
code-3.hint: Focus on the SQL task being asked for, not the final solution text.

help.concept: Build the query from the operation the exercise is testing.
help.hint_1: Eliminate choices or interpretations that do not match the task.
help.hint_2: Choose the concept that best fits what the exercise is asking you to do.

code-3.starterCode: -- Insert two rows in one statement

multi-1.title: Choosing columns for INSERT
multi-1.prompt: Which of the following statements about INSERT are true? Select all that apply.
multi-1.hint: Think about best practices and what happens if you omit columns.

help.concept: Explicitly naming columns in INSERT statements helps prevent errors and makes your code more robust. Omitting required columns without defaults causes errors.
help.hint_1: Consider what happens if you don't specify all columns, or if you rely on defaults.
help.hint_2: Some columns require values, others have defaults or allow NULL.

fill-1.title: Default values in INSERT
fill-1.prompt: When you omit the 'status' column in an INSERT into `inventory_items`, what value will it have by default?
fill-1.hint: Check the table definition for the default value of 'status'.

help.concept: Columns can have default values set in the table schema. If you don't provide a value, the default is used.
help.hint_1: Look for the DEFAULT keyword in the schema for 'status'.
help.hint_2: The default for 'status' is set in the CREATE TABLE statement.

sketch-1.title: Why INSERT matters
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
The `INSERT` statement lets you add new rows to a table. For example, to add a new inventory item:

```sql
INSERT INTO inventory_items (name, category, price, status, is_test, notes, last_updated)
VALUES ('Notebook', 'Accessories', 3.50, 'active', 0, NULL, '2026-02-10');
```

It's best practice to list the columns explicitly, so your data goes into the right places, even if the table structure changes later.

sketch-2.title: Required columns and defaults
sketch-2.bodyMarkdown: Some columns require values, while others have defaults or allow NULLs. For example, in `inventory_items`, the `status` column defaults to `'draft'` if not specified. The `notes` column can be left NULL. If you omit a required column without a default, the database will return an error.

sketch-3.title: Checking your insert
sketch-3.bodyMarkdown:
After inserting a row, you can check your work:

```sql
SELECT * FROM inventory_items WHERE name = 'Notebook';
```

This helps confirm that your new data appears as expected.
